title: Find Your Favorite Food in a Sentence

content.problemStatement:
**Long Answer - 5 marks**

In this example, we demonstrate how to use Python’s `find()` method to locate the last occurrence of a specific word ("tacos") in the sentence "I love to eat tacos, pizza, tacos, and sushi.".
This method helps you search for a substring within a larger string and returns the index where it last appears in the text.

Consider the following variable:

`p = "I love to eat tacos, pizza, tacos, and sushi."
s = "tacos"
`When the code is executed, Python will display the starting index of the last occurrence of "tacos" in the sentence.
In this example, the last "tacos" begins at index 28, so the output will be:

`28
`
Input Format

The first line of each test case contains a string **S**, which may be large.
The second line of each test case contains another string **P**, whose **last occurrence** in **S** needs to be determined.
Your task is to output the **starting index of the last occurrence** of **P** in **S**.

For example, consider the following values:


S = "I love coding and I love problem solving"

P = "love"


The substring "love" appears multiple times, and its **last occurrence** starts at index **22**, so the output will be:


22



Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the starting index of the last occurrence of the substring **P** in the string **S**.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10), the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line is the string S (may contain spaces, length ≤ 1000), the second line is the pattern string P (length ≤ 100).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the 0‑based starting index of the last occurrence of P in S. If P does not occur in S, output -1. If P is empty, output the length of S.

sampleTestCases.input:
1
I love to eat tacos, pizza, tacos, and sushi.
tacos
sampleTestCases.explanation: The pattern "tacos" appears twice. Its last occurrence starts at index 28 (0‑based).

sampleTestCases.input:
1
I love coding and I love problem solving
love
sampleTestCases.explanation: The word "love" occurs at indices 2 and 20; the last one starts at 20.
"abc" does not occur in "hello world", so the answer is -1.

solution.approach: For each test case we need the rightmost position where P appears in S. The simplest way is to scan S from the end and compare substrings of length |P|, stopping at the first match. All standard libraries provide a built‑in routine (e.g., rfind, lastIndexOf, rfind) that implements this efficiently. Edge cases: if P is empty it matches at the end of S (index = |S|); if |P| > |S| there can be no match; otherwise we return the found index or -1.
solution.timeComplexity: O(|S| * |P|) in the worst case for a manual scan; the library functions usually run in O(|S| + |P|).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) additional space besides the input strings.

code.Java:
import java.io.*;
public class Main{public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception{BufferedReader br=new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));String line=br.readLine();if(line==null)return;int T=Integer.parseInt(line.trim());StringBuilder out=new StringBuilder();for(int tc=0;tc<T;++tc){String S=br.readLine();String P=br.readLine();int ans;if(P.isEmpty()) ans=S.length();else ans=S.lastIndexOf(P);out.append(ans).append('\n');}System.out.print(out.toString());}}
